summary: Joel Spolsky announces the public launch of Stack Overflow, a programming Q&A site he co-founded with Jeff Atwood. He describes the frustrating state of existing programmer Q&A resources — paid sites, outdated Usenet posts, spam-filled forums — and explains how Stack Overflow improves on them through voting, editing, and community curation. The site treats each question like a Wikipedia article for a narrow programming topic, with answers ranked by community votes rather than chronological order. Spolsky credits the combination of his and Atwood's blog audiences for providing the critical mass needed to make the site viable from day one.
key_points: Existing programmer Q&A options were broken: paid subscription sites, outdated answers, spam-filled forums, and useful gems buried in noise
Stack Overflow's core innovation is combining Q&A with Wikipedia-style editing and Reddit-style voting to surface the best answers
Critical mass is the hardest problem for a Q&A site — Spolsky and Atwood solved it by leveraging their combined blog audiences
The site deliberately prevents discussion by ordering answers by votes rather than chronologically, forcing users to edit rather than reply
Stack Overflow is designed as a permanent, evolving resource where answers can be updated as technology changes, avoiding the problem of obsolete answers ranking high on Google
money_quotes: The hardest thing about making a new Q&A site is not the programming—it's the community.
You need a large audience of great developers so you have the critical mass it takes to get started. Without critical mass, questions go unanswered and the site becomes a ghost town.
Every question in Stack Overflow is like the Wikipedia article for some extremely narrow, specific programming question.
Already, it's better than other Q&A sites, because you don't have to read through a lot of discussion to find the right answer, if it's in there somewhere.
Indeed, you can't even have a discussion. A lot of people come to Stack Overflow, not knowing what to expect, and try to conduct a discussion when they should be answering the question.
Stack Overflow won't have the problem of other sites where obsolete or incorrect answers have high Google PageRank simply because they've been on the Internet for so long.
It costs us practically nothing to operate, so we won't need to plaster it with punch-the-monkey ads; we plan to keep it free and open to the public forever.
And it might make it a little bit easier to be a programmer.
key_insight: The key to building a useful programming Q&A site is not the technology but achieving critical mass of knowledgeable users, combined with mechanisms like voting and editing that surface the best answers and keep content current.
